Hillingdon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ealing_and_Hillingdon_\\\\(London_Assembly_constituency\\\\) \\\"Ealing and Hillingdon \\\\(London Assembly constituency\\\\)\\\")\\n\\n\\n\\nList of places\\n    [UK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_locations \\\"List of United Kingdom locations\\\")     [England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_places_in_England \\\"List of places in England\\\")     [London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_places_in_London \\\"List of places in London\\\") ![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/WMA_button2b.png/20px-WMA_button2b.png)[51\\u00b030\\u203215\\u2033N 0\\u00b027\\u203253\\u2033W\\ufeff / \\ufeff51.5043\\u00b0N 0.4646\\u00b0W\\ufeff / 51.5043; -0.4646](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=West_Drayton&params=51.5043_N_0.4646_W_region:GB_type:city\\\\(14370\\\\))  \\n**West Drayton** is a suburban town in the [London Borough of Hillingdon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London_Borough_of_Hillingdon \\\"London Borough of Hillingdon\\\"). It was an [ancient parish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_parish \\\"Ancient parish\\\") in the county of [Middlesex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middlesex \\\"Middlesex\\\") and from 1929 was part of the [Yiewsley and West Drayton Urban District](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yiewsley_and_West_Drayton_Urban_District \\\"Yiewsley and West Drayton Urban District\\\"), which became part of [Greater London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greater_London \\\"Greater London\\\") in 1965. The settlement is near the [Colne Valley Regional Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colne_Valley_Regional_Park \\\"Colne Valley Regional Park\\\") and its centre lies 1.9 miles (3 km) north of [Heathrow Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heathrow_Airport \\\"Heathrow Airport\\\"). \\nTraditionally the Parish of West Drayton covers 3.4 square kilometres (1.3 sq mi). In 1901 the population of the civil parish was 984.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-page-1) In the 2021 Census 18,433 people were living in the West Drayton [electoral ward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wards_of_the_United_Kingdom \\\"Wards of the United Kingdom\\\").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-citypopulation-2) The ward has three [councillors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Councillors \\\"Councillors\\\") in the [Hillingdon Borough Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hillingdon_London_Borough_Council \\\"Hillingdon London Borough Council\\\").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-page-1)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-3) The vast majority of the housing in West Drayton is mid-20th century. \\n## Toponymy\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=1 \\\"Edit section: Toponymy\\\")]\\nIn 939 the area was known as _Draegtun_. Tun/ton is cognate with the later form town, but originally implied any kind of farmstead of more than one family. Dray is cognate with draught (as in [draft horse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Draft_horse \\\"Draft horse\\\")/a dray) implying a portage/slope used for dragging loads, or simply notable use of the dragged plough, quite possibly given about 13 other examples in England simply land under till.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-4) It is recorded as _Draitone_ in the 1086 _[Domesday Book](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domesday_Book \\\"Domesday Book\\\")_ , and as _Westdrayton_ in 1465.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-5) It is thought that the _West_ may have been added to differentiate the village from [Drayton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Ealing \\\"West Ealing\\\") near Ealing.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-6)\\n## Geography\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=2 \\\"Edit section: Geography\\\")]\\nWest Drayton lies to the south of the [Great Western Main Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Western_Main_Line \\\"Great Western Main Line\\\") which run east\\u2013west, with [Yiewsley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yiewsley \\\"Yiewsley\\\") lying to the north of the railway line. It lies on the north side of the [M4 motorway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M4_motorway \\\"M4 motorway\\\") with the village of [Harmondsworth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmondsworth \\\"Harmondsworth\\\") to the south, and is northwest of M4 junction 4 ([Heathrow Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heathrow_Airport \\\"Heathrow Airport\\\") spur). This intersects with the A408 (for [Stockley Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stockley_Park \\\"Stockley Park\\\") and [Uxbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uxbridge \\\"Uxbridge\\\")) which forms West Drayton's eastern boundary with [Hayes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hayes,_Hillingdon \\\"Hayes, Hillingdon\\\") until the [Heathrow Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heathrow_Express \\\"Heathrow Express\\\") railway line forms this boundary at Prologos Park Heathrow. In this area lay the former hamlet of [Stockley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stockley_Park \\\"Stockley Park\\\"), known until 1912 as Starveall or Starvhall.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-7) On the eastern side of West Drayton is the county boundary with [Buckinghamshire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buckinghamshire \\\"Buckinghamshire\\\"). \\n[West Drayton railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton_railway_station \\\"West Drayton railway station\\\") is served by the [Elizabeth line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elizabeth_line \\\"Elizabeth line\\\") and [Great Western Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Western_Railway_\\\\(train_operating_company\\\\) \\\"Great Western Railway \\\\(train operating company\\\\)\\\") (GWR). The Elizabeth line operates a stopping service between [Abbey Wood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abbey_Wood \\\"Abbey Wood\\\") and [Reading](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reading_railway_station \\\"Reading railway station\\\") and GWR operates a stopping service between [London Paddington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London_Paddington_station \\\"London Paddington station\\\") and [Didcot Parkway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Didcot_Parkway_railway_station \\\"Didcot Parkway railway station\\\"). \\nWest Drayton has five primary schools, West Drayton Academy, Laurel Lane Primary School, St Martin's CE Primary School, St Catherine Catholic Primary School and Cherry Lane Primary School. The community is served by the [Park Academy West London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Park_Academy_West_London \\\"Park Academy West London\\\") secondary school which is located on Park View Road in Yiewsley. \\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/West_Drayton_The_Green_1.jpg/250px-West_Drayton_The_Green_1.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:West_Drayton_The_Green_1.jpg)The Green\\nWest Drayton has at its heart in the west of the parish a [conservation area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_area \\\"Conservation area\\\"), The Green, along which are many buildings protected under UK law by [grade II and II* listing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Listed_building \\\"Listed building\\\") \\u2013 residential and commercial. \\n## Demography\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=3 \\\"Edit section: Demography\\\")]\\nAs of the 2011 census, 57% of the population was White British.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-8) 18,433 people were living in the ward according to the 2021 census.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-citypopulation-2)\\nThe decennial censuses between 1801 and 1901 each show a rise in population and a low population density. From 1801 when the population of the almost identically sized ecclesiastical parish (civil parishes were invented later in the 19th century) was 515; to 1901 when the population of the civil parish was 984.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-page-1)\\n## Politics\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=4 \\\"Edit section: Politics\\\")]\\nWest Drayton is part of the [Hayes and Harlington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hayes_and_Harlington_\\\\(UK_Parliament_constituency\\\\) \\\"Hayes and Harlington \\\\(UK Parliament constituency\\\\)\\\") constituency for elections to the [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom \\\"House of Commons of the United Kingdom\\\"). \\nWest Drayton is part of the [West Drayton ward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton_\\\\(ward\\\\) \\\"West Drayton \\\\(ward\\\\)\\\") for elections to [Hillingdon London Borough Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hillingdon_London_Borough_Council \\\"Hillingdon London Borough Council\\\").[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-9)\\n## History\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=5 \\\"Edit section: History\\\")]\\nSee also: [Elthorne Hundred](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elthorne_Hundred \\\"Elthorne Hundred\\\") and [Middlesex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middlesex \\\"Middlesex\\\")\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=6 \\\"Edit section: St Paul's era 939\\u20131546\\\")]\\nThe first record of West Drayton is from 939 when [\\u00c6thelstan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%86thelstan \\\"\\u00c6thelstan\\\"), King of the English, gave the Manor of West Drayton to the Dean and Chapter of Cathedral church of [St Pauls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Cathedral \\\"St Paul's Cathedral\\\"), recorded in the [Cartularium Saxonicum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cartularium_Saxonicum \\\"Cartularium Saxonicum\\\").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Cartularium-10) In the 1086 _[Domesday Book](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domesday_Book \\\"Domesday Book\\\")_ , West Drayton was assessed at ten hides with land suitable for six ploughs.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-11) The Parish had 17 landowners which indicated a population of less than 100.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) In 1461 a separate smaller manor, Drayton and Colham Garden Manor was first recorded, lying between Swan road and Colham Mill road.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13) Both manors shared St Martin's Parish Church. Until 1525 West Drayton Manor was managed on behalf of the Dean and Chapter of St Paul's by an appointment known as a _Firmarius_ who was responsible for the day-to-day running of the estate.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=7 \\\"Edit section: Paget era 1546\\u20131786\\\")]\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/William_Paget%2C_1st_Baron_Paget_by_Master_of_the_St%C3%A4tthalterin_Madonna.jpg/250px-William_Paget%2C_1st_Baron_Paget_by_Master_of_the_St%C3%A4tthalterin_Madonna.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:William_Paget,_1st_Baron_Paget_by_Master_of_the_St%C3%A4tthalterin_Madonna.jpg)[William Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_1st_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 1st Baron Paget\\\"), 1st Baron Paget.\\nFrom 1525 St Paul's began leasing West Drayton Manor to tenants. From 1537 the lessee of the manor was [William Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_1st_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 1st Baron Paget\\\") who held high office of state in the court of [Henry VIII](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_VIII \\\"Henry VIII\\\"). On 1 April 1546, the Dean and Chapter of St Paul's released the Manor of West Drayton to the Crown and two weeks later Henry granted the manor to the now knighted Sir William Paget.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) In the same year Henry granted Paget six manors in his native Staffordshire.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Hawkyard_History_of_Parliament-14) Henry died in January 1547. Paget was made a peer of the realm as Lord Paget of Beaudesert by [Edward VI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_VI \\\"Edward VI\\\") in 1549.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Hawkyard_History_of_Parliament-14)\\nWhile he was leasing the manor William Paget used the existing building of St Pauls which was situated near St Martin's Parish church. Paget described the building as his _\\\"cotage at Drayton\\\"_.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) Once owning the manor he built a new manor house, completed by 1549, which occupied the western end of the churchyard. The Manor grounds contained the Church, ornamental gardens, stables, a dovecote and other outbuildings and was enclosed by a high brick wall and two gatehouses.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13) The wall and one of the gatehouses can still be seen today. The construction of the manor house and grounds resulted in the demolition of villagers' homes on Church Road and building on the graves of generations of West Drayton people in the churchyard. In addition to this, Paget enclosed 150 acres of common land to add to his [demesne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demesne \\\"Demesne\\\"). In 1550 Paget legalised his position by obtaining a royal pardon for his actions.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) The loss of the Parish churchyard was compensated for by the granting of an alternative burial site which was situated on the eastern side of where Drayton Hall is today. The burial site was used until 1888.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/West_Drayton_The_Gatehouse_2.jpg/250px-West_Drayton_The_Gatehouse_2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:West_Drayton_The_Gatehouse_2.jpg)Gatehouse to the former West Drayton Manor House\\nAfter serving as [Lord Privy Seal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Privy_Seal \\\"Lord Privy Seal\\\") under [Mary I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_I \\\"Mary I\\\"), poor health meant William Paget played little part in public life after the accession of [Queen Elizabeth I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I \\\"Queen Elizabeth I\\\") in 1558, although as a [Privy Councillor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom \\\"Privy Council of the United Kingdom\\\") his advice was often sought.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Hawkyard_History_of_Parliament-14) He died in West Drayton on 9 June 1563.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Hawkyard_History_of_Parliament-14) He was succeeded by his son [Henry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_2nd_Baron_Paget \\\"Henry Paget, 2nd Baron Paget\\\"), who died five years later without male [issue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Issue_\\\\(genealogy\\\\) \\\"Issue \\\\(genealogy\\\\)\\\"). Henry's brother [Thomas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Paget,_3rd_Baron_Paget \\\"Thomas Paget, 3rd Baron Paget\\\") became the 3rd Baron Paget in 1568. \\n[Thomas Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Paget,_3rd_Baron_Paget \\\"Thomas Paget, 3rd Baron Paget\\\") (1544\\u20131590) and his brother [Charles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Paget_\\\\(conspirator\\\\) \\\"Charles Paget \\\\(conspirator\\\\)\\\") were both devout Roman Catholics, and would not conform to the Protestant religion of [Queen Elizabeth I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I \\\"Queen Elizabeth I\\\"). Aided by [Henry Percy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Percy,_8th_Earl_of_Northumberland \\\"Henry Percy, 8th Earl of Northumberland\\\"), Paget fled to Paris on the uncovering of the [Throckmorton Plot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Throckmorton_Plot \\\"Throckmorton Plot\\\") in November 1583, joining Charles who had been in exile there since 1581.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Sir_Philip_Sidney-15) The failed conspiracy's plan was for an invasion of England by French forces under the command of [Henry, Duke of Guise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_I,_Duke_of_Guise \\\"Henry I, Duke of Guise\\\"), financed by [Philip II of Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain \\\"Philip II of Spain\\\"). English Catholics would then rise up and depose Elizabeth, placing Catholic [Mary, Queen of Scots](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots \\\"Mary, Queen of Scots\\\") on the English throne. \\nAfter his flight to France Elizabeth issued a [proclamation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proclamation \\\"Proclamation\\\") commanding Thomas Paget to return to England. In June 1584 a formal demand for the surrender of Paget was made to [Henry III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_III_of_France \\\"Henry III of France\\\"), King of France through the English ambassador, which was not carried out. \\nIn 1584 or 1585 Philip II made the decision for Spain to invade England and depose Queen Elizabeth. The ground invasion would be led by [Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma \\\"Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma\\\") with the majority of troops coming from the [Spanish Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands \\\"Spanish Netherlands\\\"). Parma commanded the [Army of Flanders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Army_of_Flanders \\\"Army of Flanders\\\") which was engaged in fighting England's Protestant Dutch allies. \\nPaget travelled to Spain and was given a pension of 180 crowns per month by Philip II.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-ODNB-16) He then moved to the Spanish Netherlands where he was consulted by Parma in the planning of the invasion.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-ODNB-16) The endeavour of July\\u2013August 1588 to stop England's rise as a maritime power and to force the English and Welsh back to Roman Catholicism failed when the [Spanish Armada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spanish_Armada \\\"Spanish Armada\\\") required to support Parma's passage from the Spanish Netherlands to England was scattered after engagements with the English Navy off the coast of [Calais](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calais \\\"Calais\\\") and [Gravelines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravelines \\\"Gravelines\\\"). Thomas Paget would die in Brussels in 1590.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-ODNB-16)\\nIn 1587 Thomas Paget had been [attainted](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_of_attainder \\\"Bill of attainder\\\") of treason by act of parliament. His lands including West Drayton were confiscated by the Crown.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13) Elizabeth granted the manor to her [Lord Chancellor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Chancellor \\\"Lord Chancellor\\\"), [Sir Christopher Hatton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christopher_Hatton \\\"Christopher Hatton\\\") for life. Hatton died in 1591 and from 1592 the manor was leased to her [Lord Chamberlain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Chamberlain \\\"Lord Chamberlain\\\"), [George Carey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Carey,_2nd_Baron_Hunsdon \\\"George Carey, 2nd Baron Hunsdon\\\"). Carey became 2nd Baron Hunsdon in 1596 and entertained Elizabeth at the West Drayton Manor House in October 1602.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\nIn the next year 1603, both Elizabeth and George Carey died. With Elizabeth's death, [James I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_VI_and_I \\\"James VI and I\\\"), son of Mary Queen of Scots acceded to the English throne. In 1604 James restored the Paget family lands and honours to Thomas Paget's son [William](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_4th_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 4th Baron Paget\\\").[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13) William like James I was a Protestant and had taken part in the successful Anglo-Dutch [capture of C\\u00e1diz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capture_of_C%C3%A1diz \\\"Capture of C\\u00e1diz\\\") (Gades) in 1596 with [Admiral Charles Howard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Nottingham \\\"Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Nottingham\\\"), [Sir Walter Raleigh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_Raleigh \\\"Walter Raleigh\\\") and [Robert Devereux, 2nd Earl of Essex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Devereux,_2nd_Earl_of_Essex \\\"Robert Devereux, 2nd Earl of Essex\\\").[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-17)[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-18) It is believed William Paget received possession of West Drayton Manor in 1610.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13) In 1612 William Paget became an 'Adventurer' (shareholder) and member of the Council of the [Virginia Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virginia_Company \\\"Virginia Company\\\") ([London Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London_Company \\\"London Company\\\")) and the [Somers Isles Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Somers_Isles_Company \\\"Somers Isles Company\\\"). The [Paget Parish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paget_Parish \\\"Paget Parish\\\") and [Paget Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paget_Island \\\"Paget Island\\\") in Bermuda are named after him. He died on 29 August 1629 and is buried in the vault of St Martin's church. \\nWilliam Paget's son, [William Paget, 5th Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_5th_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 5th Baron Paget\\\"), (1609\\u20131678) was among the [Peers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peerages_in_the_United_Kingdom \\\"Peerages in the United Kingdom\\\") who petitioned [King Charles I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_I_of_England \\\"Charles I of England\\\") on 18 August 1640 to summon a parliament for the redress of grievances. However at the start of the [Civil War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_Civil_War \\\"English Civil War\\\") he did not wish to take up arms against the king and joined him at York in June 1642.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-The_Complete_Peerage-19) He raised a regiment of foot which fought for the King at the [battle of Edgehill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill \\\"Battle of Edgehill\\\") on 23 Oct 1642. In 1643/44 he was with the King at Oxford.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-The_Complete_Peerage-19) Paget had his estates [sequestered](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequestration_\\\\(law\\\\) \\\"Sequestration \\\\(law\\\\)\\\") by Parliament and was fined \\u00a3500 for supporting the King.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) He died in October 1678 and is buried in the vault of St Martin's church. \\nWilliam's son, [William Paget, 6th Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_6th_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 6th Baron Paget\\\"), (1637\\u20131713) was appointed by [William III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_III_of_England \\\"William III of England\\\") as English Ambassador to Vienna (1689\\u20131692) and Ambassador-Extraordinary to Constantinople (1692\\u20131702).[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-archiveshub.jisc.ac.uk-20) He was joint chairman at the congress of Karlowitz which resulted in the [Treaty of Karlowitz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Treaty_of_Karlowitz \\\"Treaty of Karlowitz\\\") of 26 January 1699 which brought peace between the [Holy League](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holy_League_\\\\(1684\\\\) \\\"Holy League \\\\(1684\\\\)\\\") and the [Ottoman Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottoman_Empire \\\"Ottoman Empire\\\") after the 15 year [Great Turkish War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Turkish_War \\\"Great Turkish War\\\").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-ODNB_7th_Baron-21)\\n[![Arms of Paget](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Arms_of_Paget.svg/250px-Arms_of_Paget.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Arms_of_Paget.svg)\\nThe Paget coat of arms\\n[![Hillingdon coat of Arms](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Coat_of_Arms_of_the_London_Borough_of_Hillingdon.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_the_London_Borough_of_Hillingdon.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_the_London_Borough_of_Hillingdon.svg)\\n[The Paget heraldic eagle in the coat of arms of the London Borough of Hillingdon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_London_Borough_of_Hillingdon \\\"Coat of arms of the London Borough of Hillingdon\\\")\\nIn 1714 William's son, Henry Paget the 7th Lord Paget,(1663\\u20131743), was created [1st Earl of Uxbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_1st_Earl_of_Uxbridge_\\\\(first_creation\\\\) \\\"Henry Paget, 1st Earl of Uxbridge \\\\(first creation\\\\)\\\") by [George I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain \\\"George I of Great Britain\\\"). Paget was a member of the [Privy Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_Council \\\"Privy Council\\\") and the town of [Uxbridge, Massachusetts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uxbridge,_Massachusetts \\\"Uxbridge, Massachusetts\\\") is named in his honour. He died at West Drayton on 30 August 1743 and was buried at Hillingdon Church.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-ODNB_1st_Earl_of_Uxbridge-22)\\nHenry Paget was succeeded by his grandson, [Henry Paget, 2nd Earl of Uxbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_2nd_Earl_of_Uxbridge \\\"Henry Paget, 2nd Earl of Uxbridge\\\") (1719\\u20131769). In 1755 Dawley House was acquired and the West Drayton Manor house was demolished around this time. Henry Paget died childless on 16 November 1769 and was buried in the vault of St Martin's Church. The title of 9th Baron Paget was given to [Henry Bayly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_1st_Earl_of_Uxbridge_\\\\(second_creation\\\\) \\\"Henry Paget, 1st Earl of Uxbridge \\\\(second creation\\\\)\\\"), whose name was changed to Henry Paget by Royal License in 1770. He was created 1st Earl of Uxbridge (Second creation) in May 1784. On 21 October 1786 Henry Bayly-Paget sold the Manor of West Drayton to Fysh Burgh (Born Fysh Coppinger) of Lincoln's Inn, for \\u00a312,000 ending the Paget family's relationship with West Drayton.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23)\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=8 \\\"Edit section: De Burgh era 1786\\u20131939\\\")]\\nFysh Burgh, a London merchant, purchased West Drayton Manor in 1786.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) He had been born Fysh Coppinger, but took the name Burgh sometime after 1773 by proving descent from [ Sir Thomas Burgh of Gainsborough](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Burgh_of_Gainsborough \\\"Thomas Burgh of Gainsborough\\\"). In December 1790 he adopted the form De Burgh.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) As the West Drayton manor house had been demolished around 1755 he took the seventeenth century mansion, Drayton Hall as his family home.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\nFysh De Burgh died in Bath on 14 January 1800 and was buried in the family vault in St Martin's church.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) His only son had died in January 1793 so he held his estates in trust for his married daughter Catherine, who assumed the name De Burgh.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) Fysh De Burgh's widow, Easter De Burgh, held a life interest in the estates and on her death in 1823 the manor passed to her grandson, Hubert De Burgh.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) Hubert's Lordship of the Manor would last nearly fifty years.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) He became good friends with ex-Emperor of the French [Napoleon III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napoleon_III \\\"Napoleon III\\\") becoming his [Master of the Horse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master_of_the_Horse \\\"Master of the Horse\\\").[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-25) Hubert entertained Napoleon III at Drayton Hall in 1872.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) The visit is recalled in the name of Napoleon Cottages on Money Lane.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) Hubert De Burgh died at his London house in [Hans Place](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hans_Place \\\"Hans Place\\\") on 25 September 1872.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-26) He was buried in the De Burgh family vault in St Martin's Church on 10 October 1872.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-27) The Uxbridge Yeomanry Cavalry which he had commanded, escorted his coffin from Drayton Hall to the church where the 24th Uxbridge Rifles fired a salute.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\nThe Lordship of the Manor then passed to Hubert's son, Francis de Burgh who served in the [11th Hussars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/11th_Hussars \\\"11th Hussars\\\"). Francis would die without issue in 1874 at the age of 35.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) The manor passed jointly to his two sisters Minna Edith Elizabeth and Eva Elizabeth.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23)\\nIn the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries the De Burgh estates were sold off gradually, mainly for building purposes.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-National_Archives-23) Eva Elizabeth De Burgh was sole owner of Drayton Hall when she died unmarried on 4 February 1939 at the age of 87.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-28) She was buried in the vault of St Martin's church on 8 February 1939 ending the 152 year De Burgh family connection with West Drayton.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-29)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-30)\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Middlesex_parishes.jpg/960px-Middlesex_parishes.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Middlesex_parishes.jpg \\\"The Parish of West Drayton within Middlesex in the early 19th century\\\")\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Middlesex_parishes.jpg/960px-Middlesex_parishes.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Middlesex_parishes.jpg)\\n[ ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Middlesex_parishes.jpg \\\"File:Middlesex parishes.jpg\\\")\\nThe Parish of West Drayton within Middlesex in the early 19th century\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=9 \\\"Edit section: St Martin's Church\\\")]\\nWest Drayton's parish church, dedicated to [Martin of Tours](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_of_Tours \\\"Martin of Tours\\\"), was first mentioned in the 12th century.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-St_Martins-31) However, no trace of the original church remains. The present church was first built in the 13th century, of which the base of the tower, the piscina and the north chancel wall are incorporated in the present building, which dates from the 15th century. The church was heavily restored and reordered in 1974, when the altar was resited at the west end. The baptismal font is a splendid example of mid-15th century work, and the parish chest is early 17th century. There are monumental brasses to Richard Roose (1406), Margaret Burnell (1529), her son John Burnell (1551) and Dr James Good (1581, a physician to [Mary, Queen of Scots](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots \\\"Mary, Queen of Scots\\\")). There is a small memorial tablet to [George Carey, 2nd Baron Hunsdon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Carey,_2nd_Baron_Hunsdon \\\"George Carey, 2nd Baron Hunsdon\\\") (1547\\u20131603) who was Lord of West Drayton Manor between 1592 and 1603.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-32) Carey's father was Queen Elizabeth I's cousin. The memorial states how 'bountiful' he was to the poor of the Parish. In his will Hunsdon left the sum of 'one hundred marks for the benefit of the poor of West Drayton for ever.' This is one of the oldest West Drayton Parish charities and its small income is still distributed each New Year's Day.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=10 \\\"Edit section: St Catherine's Catholic Church\\\")]\\nMain article: [St Catherine's Church, West Drayton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Catherine%27s_Church,_West_Drayton \\\"St Catherine's Church, West Drayton\\\")\\nAfter the Potato Famine of the 1840s _(known in Ireland as the[Great Famine or Great Hunger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Famine_\\\\(Ireland\\\\) \\\"Great Famine \\\\(Ireland\\\\)\\\"))_, Irish immigrants who were of the [Roman Catholic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Catholic \\\"Roman Catholic\\\") religion and principally from counties Cork and Waterford arrived in West Drayton and took up residence in poor housing by The Green which became known locally as the Irish hovels.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-33) Today this is the site of Daisy Villas.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) Work for the Irish immigrants was found in the market-gardens surrounding West Drayton.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Rev._Wren-34)\\nThe nearest official place of Catholic worship was St. Mary's chapel at North Hyde, requiring a walk of over four miles each way to attend Mass on a Sunday.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) Because of this distance a Roman Catholic Mass was offered in a stable at the back of the Kings Head Public House adjacent to The Green.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-35) By 1862 the Reverend Andrew Mooney, priest at the St. Mary's North Hyde Orphanage was using a cottage in Money Lane called the 'White House' as a school and was conducting services there.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12) He was succeeded by the Reverend Peter Francis Elkins, also the priest at the North Hyde Mission to which the school at West Drayton was attached.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-St_Catherine_50_years-36)[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-37)\\nOn 30 May 1867 West Drayton became a Catholic Mission with the Rev. Elkins becoming the resident priest.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-38) In July 1867 Rev. Elkins petitioned the [Archbishop of Westminster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archbishop_of_Westminster \\\"Archbishop of Westminster\\\") to support the building of a church and school.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-39) The building of a church and school was given the [special approbation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Approbation_\\\\(Catholic_canon_law\\\\) \\\"Approbation \\\\(Catholic canon law\\\\)\\\") of the Archbishop.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-40)\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Roman_Catholic_Church_of_St_Catherine%2C_West_Drayton_%2801%29.jpg/250px-Roman_Catholic_Church_of_St_Catherine%2C_West_Drayton_%2801%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Roman_Catholic_Church_of_St_Catherine,_West_Drayton_\\\\(01\\\\).jpg)St Catherine's Church\\nBy September 1867 Rev. Elkins health had failed and the West Drayton Mission was undertaken by Reverend Michael Wren.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-41) Through the autumn, winter and spring of 1867-8 Rev. Wren requested financial donations for the new church and school stating that West Drayton was the most destitute mission in England with upwards of 300 persons being compelled to remain in the open air during Catholic mass.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-42)\\nOn 19 December 1867 a house and land for the church and school was purchased at public auction for \\u00a3855.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-43)[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-St_Catherine_50_years-36) Rev. Wren used the house as his Presbytery and a temporary chapel was created in the adjoining coach-house and stables.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Rev._Wren-34) Premises at the back of the property were demolished to make room for the school.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-44)[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-St_Catherine_50_years-36)\\nOn 26 October 1868 the [Most Reverend Dr. Henry Manning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Edward_Manning \\\"Henry Edward Manning\\\"), Archbishop of Westminster laid the foundation stone of the [Church of St Catherine of Alexandria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Catherine%27s_Church,_West_Drayton \\\"St Catherine's Church, West Drayton\\\").[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-45) On 29 September 1869 he opened the church, preaching its first sermon.[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-46)\\nDespite Rev. Wren's efforts in seeking donations, a loan was required to fund the building of the church and school.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Rev._Wren-34) This debt resulted in the church not being consecrated until 29 September 1893, twenty-four years after its opening.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-47) Both the opening and consecration of the church occurred on 29 September, [Michaelmas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michaelmas \\\"Michaelmas\\\"), the feast of St. Michael, Rev. Michael Wren's patron saint.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\nThe church is built of buff stock brick with Bath stone dressing with dimensions of 85ft by 48ft and was designed by architects Samuel Joseph Nicholl and Thomas John Willson of London in the English Gothic style of the early 14th century.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-48) It was fitted to accommodate 500 persons and was constructed by builders James and William Fassnidge of Uxbridge.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-49) The schoolroom was 40ft long and 20ft broad and accommodated 250 children and was in use until 1939.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-50)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n## Local economy\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=11 \\\"Edit section: Local economy\\\")]\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/West_Drayton_Drayton_Hall_2.jpg/250px-West_Drayton_Drayton_Hall_2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:West_Drayton_Drayton_Hall_2.jpg)Drayton Hall, offices of Ferring Pharmaceuticals, Kore Wireless and North South Wines\\nHistorically, employment was commonly connected to agriculture, the railway yards and the canal. The former [RAF West Drayton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RAF_West_Drayton \\\"RAF West Drayton\\\") hosted a military [air traffic control](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air_traffic_control \\\"Air traffic control\\\") centre co-located with the civil [London Terminal Control Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London_Terminal_Control_Centre \\\"London Terminal Control Centre\\\"), residual functions of which were relocated to [Swanwick, Hampshire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swanwick,_Hampshire \\\"Swanwick, Hampshire\\\") entirely by January 2008. \\nWest Drayton has a mixture of tradespeople, airport workers, construction workers and [commuters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commuting \\\"Commuting\\\") in office professions or public utilities such as schools and hospitals. Its housing hosts people with a range of incomes, with relatively large areas built aesthetically in keeping with existing housing by the local authority. \\nWest Drayton has rapid connectivity to the [M4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M4_motorway \\\"M4 motorway\\\") and thereafter to the [M25](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M25_motorway \\\"M25 motorway\\\") and motorways north-west and south-west. The M4 spur and the A408 Stockley Road by-pass links to [Heathrow Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heathrow_Airport \\\"Heathrow Airport\\\") and to the [Stockley Business Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stockley_Park \\\"Stockley Park\\\"), respectively. The Stockley Close Industrial estate lies on the eastern side of the by-pass. The businesses situated here are: Ocado, Greencore, Carrier Retail Systems, Amalga Ltd, Clevertronics, MNX Global Logistics and the Heathrow Parcel Centre. \\nDrayton Hall has the offices of Ferring Pharmaceuticals, Trizell Ltd, Kore Wireless and North South Wines. Britannia Court, on the east side of The Green, has the offices of the Schools HR Co-operative, MagLabs, Wells Burcombe LLP, MD Developments Ltd, Insultec Ltd, QIK Group and Flight Data Systems. \\n## Culture and recreation\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=12 \\\"Edit section: Culture and recreation\\\")]\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/West_Drayton_Southlands_3.jpg/250px-West_Drayton_Southlands_3.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:West_Drayton_Southlands_3.jpg)Southlands Arts Centre\\n  * The Yiewsley and West Drayton Arts Council maintain the [Southlands Arts Centre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southlands_Arts_Centre \\\"Southlands Arts Centre\\\").[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-51) They oversee events and promote local creativity. There are many exhibitions, music festivals, and creative endeavours from fine arts, photography, film-making and music groups.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-52)\\n  * Community events and activities are held at the Yiewsley and West Drayton Community centre on Harmondsworth Road.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-53)\\n  * West Drayton Library is situated on Station Road.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Library-54)\\n  * The Yiewsley and West Drayton Band is a second section brass band established in 1890 and maintains a year-round programme of concerts and community events.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-55)\\n  * 1381 Squadron (West Drayton & Yiewsley) Royal Air Force Air Cadets are based at Summer Drive.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-56)\\n  * The 2nd West Drayton Scout Group are based at Rowan Road.\\n  * There are two local amateur [football](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_football \\\"Association football\\\") clubs, West Drayton FC and Townmead Youth FC.\\n  * The Closes public park has a playground, outdoor gym, Tennis courts and a multi use ball court.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-57)\\n\\n[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Mabey%27s_Meadow.jpg/250px-Mabey%27s_Meadow.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Mabey%27s_Meadow.jpg)Mabey's Meadow in the Frays Island and Mabey's Meadow Nature Reserve\\n  * The [Colne Valley regional park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colne_Valley_regional_park \\\"Colne Valley regional park\\\") lies on the western side of West Drayton. Here Frays Island and Mabey's Meadow are [Sites of Metropolitan Importance for Nature Conservation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Site_of_Nature_Conservation_Interest \\\"Site of Nature Conservation Interest\\\") and are managed by the London Wildlife Trust.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Frays_Island-58)\\n\\n\\n## Notable people\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=13 \\\"Edit section: Notable people\\\")]\\n  * Sir [William Paget, 1st Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_1st_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 1st Baron Paget\\\") [KG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter \\\"Order of the Garter\\\") [PC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_council \\\"Privy council\\\") (1506\\u20131563), statesman, holder of high office in the courts of [Henry VIII](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_VIII \\\"Henry VIII\\\"), [Edward VI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_VI \\\"Edward VI\\\"), [Mary I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_I \\\"Mary I\\\") and [Elizabeth I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elizabeth_I \\\"Elizabeth I\\\"). Lived in West Drayton from 1537 until his death in 1563 and is buried in St Martin's Churchyard.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13)\\n  * [Thomas Paget, 3rd Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Paget,_3rd_Baron_Paget \\\"Thomas Paget, 3rd Baron Paget\\\") (1544\\u20131590), [attainted of treason](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_of_attainder \\\"Bill of attainder\\\"), was Lord of West Drayton Manor 1568\\u20131587.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-BHO_VCH_West_Drayton_Manors-13)\\n  * Sir [Christopher Hatton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christopher_Hatton \\\"Christopher Hatton\\\"), [Lord Chancellor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Chancellor \\\"Lord Chancellor\\\") [KG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter \\\"Order of the Garter\\\") [PC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_council \\\"Privy council\\\") (1540\\u20131591), was Lord of West Drayton Manor 1587\\u20131591.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\n  * Sir [George Carey, 2nd Baron Hunsdon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Carey,_2nd_Baron_Hunsdon \\\"George Carey, 2nd Baron Hunsdon\\\") [Lord Chamberlain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_Chamberlain \\\"Lord Chamberlain\\\") [KG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter \\\"Order of the Garter\\\") [PC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_council \\\"Privy council\\\") (1547\\u20131603), lived at West Drayton between 1592 and his death in 1603.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-A.H._Cox_1983-12)\\n  * Sir [William Paget, 4th Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_4th_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 4th Baron Paget\\\") (1572\\u20131629), 'Adventurer' (shareholder) and member of the Council of the [Virginia Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virginia_Company \\\"Virginia Company\\\") ([London Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/London_Company \\\"London Company\\\")) and the [Somers Isles Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Somers_Isles_Company \\\"Somers Isles Company\\\") is buried in St Martin's Churchyard.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Bermuda-59)\\n  * Sir [William Paget, 5th Baron Paget](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Paget,_5th_Baron_Paget \\\"William Paget, 5th Baron Paget\\\") [KB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath \\\"Order of the Bath\\\") (1609\\u20131678), Royalist during the English Civil War, is buried in St Martin's Churchyard.\\n  * [Henry Paget, 1st Earl of Uxbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_1st_Earl_of_Uxbridge_\\\\(first_creation\\\\) \\\"Henry Paget, 1st Earl of Uxbridge \\\\(first creation\\\\)\\\") [PC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Privy_council \\\"Privy council\\\") (1663\\u20131743) died at West Drayton Manor.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-The_Complete_Peerage-19)\\n  * Sir [Henry Paget, 2nd Earl of Uxbridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Paget,_2nd_Earl_of_Uxbridge \\\"Henry Paget, 2nd Earl of Uxbridge\\\") (1719\\u20131769), is buried in St Martin's Churchyard.\\n  * Publisher [Sir Allen Lane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sir_Allen_Lane \\\"Sir Allen Lane\\\") founder of Penguin Books, lived at The Mill House.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n  * [Sir Charles Aubrey Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C_Aubrey_Smith \\\"C Aubrey Smith\\\") Captain of the England Cricket team and Hollywood actor, lived at Avenue House between 1903 and 1908 and the Old Orchard in Mill Road from 1908 to 1930.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n  * [William Nigel Bruce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nigel_Bruce \\\"Nigel Bruce\\\") Hollywood actor, lived at 1 De Burgh Crescent between 1905 and 1934.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n  * [Henry Havelock Ellis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Havelock_Ellis \\\"Havelock Ellis\\\") physician and writer, lived at Woodpecker Farm, Mill Road between 1911 and 1913.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n  * [Cosmo Hamilton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmo_Hamilton \\\"Cosmo Hamilton\\\") Playwright and Novelist, lived at Southlands.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Southlands_history-60)\\n  * [J.W. (Jack) Hearne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.W._Hearne \\\"J.W. Hearne\\\"), Cricketer, who represented England in twenty-four Test matches, lived in Bagley Close.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n  * Actor [Julian Rhind-Tutt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julian_Rhind-Tutt \\\"Julian Rhind-Tutt\\\") was born in West Drayton.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_note-Skinner_West_Drayton_and_Yiewsley-24)\\n\\n\\n## References\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Drayton&action=edit&section=14 \\\"Edit section: References\\\")]\\n  1. ^ [Jump up to: _**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_ref-page_1-0) [_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_ref-page_1-1) [_**c**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Drayton#cite_ref-page_1-2) 838 acres. UK Postcode District (Most Common Usage)\n\n**UB7** is a postcode district in the **UB postcode area** of Greater London, England. Here are the key details:\n\n### Location & Coverage\n- **Primary Area**: West Drayton, a suburban town in the London Borough of Hillingdon\n- **Coverage Area**: West Drayton, Harmondsworth, Sipson, Yiewsley, Longford, and parts of Hillingdon\n- **Region**: Greater London (Outer London)\n- **Local Authority**: Hillingdon London Borough Council\n- **Post Town**: WEST DRAYTON\n\n### Key Characteristics\n- **Population**: Approximately 14,370 people (West Drayton town)\n- **Location**: About 14.8 miles west of Charing Cross, central London\n- **Proximity**: Near Heathrow Airport (approximately 1.9 miles north)\n- **Transportation**: \n  - West Drayton railway station (served by Elizabeth line and Great Western Railway)\n  - Close to M4 motorway and M25\n\n### Demographics & Housing\n- Average house price: approximately £270,000 (slightly below UK average)\n- Population density: 363.6 people per sq km\n- Diverse community with mix of airport workers, tradespeople, and commuters\n- Majority of housing is mid-20th century\n\n### Notable Features\n- Near Colne Valley Regional Park\n- Conservation area known as \"The Green\" with Grade II and II* listed buildings\n- Former site of RAF West Drayton (military air traffic control centre)\n\n---\n\n## 2. SM UB-7 German Submarine (Historical)\n\n**SM UB-7** was a German Type UB I submarine from World War I:\n\n### Key Details\n- **Type**: German Type UB I coastal submarine\n- **Commissioned**: May 6, 1915\n- **Builder**: Germaniawerft, Kiel\n- **Displacement**: 127 tonnes (surfaced), 142 tonnes (submerged)\n- **Length**: 28.10 meters (92 ft 2 in)\n- **Armament**: 2 × 45 cm torpedo tubes, 2 torpedoes, 1 × 8 mm machine gun\n- **Complement**: 14 crew members\n\n### Service History\n- Operated primarily in the Black Sea as part of the Constantinople Flotilla\n- Sank 4 merchant ships totaling 6,283 GRT (Gross Register Tonnage)\n- Most notable victory: British steamer *Patagonia* (6,011 GRT) on September 18, 1915\n- Helped repel Russian bombardment of Bulgaria in October 1915\n\n### Fate\n- Disappeared on or after September 27, 1916, while operating off Sevastopol\n- Official fate: Unknown\n- Possible causes: Struck a mine or sunk by Russian aircraft\n- 15 crew members lost, including the Constantinople Flotilla's senior radio officer\n\n---\n\n## Other Minor Uses\n\n**Music/Entertainment**:\n- #UB7 is also used as a stage name by a Bulgarian music group (Urban Boys Sofia) with presence on YouTube, Spotify, and Apple Music\n- The group produces music in genres like hip-hop and trap\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\nThe term **UB7** most commonly refers to the **postcode district in West Drayton, London**, which is a residential and commercial area near Heathrow Airport. Historically, it also designates a **World War I German submarine** that served in the Black Sea. patrols[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB-7-4)  \\nVictories | 4 merchant ships sunk   \\n(6,283 [GRT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage \\\"Gross register tonnage\\\"))[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB-7-4)  \\n**SM _UB-7_**[[Note 1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-9) was a German [Type UB I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Type_UB_I_submarine \\\"Type UB I submarine\\\") [submarine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Submarine \\\"Submarine\\\") or [U-boat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U-boat \\\"U-boat\\\") in the [German Imperial Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Imperial_Navy \\\"German Imperial Navy\\\") ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language \\\"German language\\\"): _Kaiserliche Marine_) during [World War I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_I \\\"World War I\\\"). She disappeared in the [Black Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Sea \\\"Black Sea\\\") in September 1916. \\n_UB-7_ was ordered in October 1914 and was [laid down](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keel_laying \\\"Keel laying\\\") at the [AG Weser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AG_Weser \\\"AG Weser\\\") shipyard in [Bremen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bremen \\\"Bremen\\\") in November. _UB-7_ was a little over 28 metres (92 ft) in length and [displaced](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Displacement_\\\\(ship\\\\) \\\"Displacement \\\\(ship\\\\)\\\") between 127 and 141 tonnes (125 and 139 long tons), depending on whether surfaced or submerged. She carried two [torpedoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torpedo \\\"Torpedo\\\") for her two bow [torpedo tubes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torpedo_tube \\\"Torpedo tube\\\") and was also armed with a deck-mounted [machine gun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Machine_gun \\\"Machine gun\\\"). _UB-7_ was originally one of a pair of UB I boats sent to the [Austro-Hungarian Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Navy \\\"Austro-Hungarian Navy\\\") to replace an Austrian pair to be sent to the [Dardanelles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dardanelles \\\"Dardanelles\\\"), and was broken into sections and shipped by rail to [Pola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pula \\\"Pula\\\") in March 1915 for reassembly. She was [launched](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Launch_\\\\(ship\\\\) \\\"Launch \\\\(ship\\\\)\\\") in April and [commissioned](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ship_commissioning \\\"Ship commissioning\\\") as SM _UB-7_ in the German Imperial Navy in May when the Austrians opted out of the agreement. \\nAlthough briefly a part of the [Pola Flotilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pola_Flotilla \\\"Pola Flotilla\\\") at commissioning, _UB-7_ spent the majority of her career patrolling the [Black Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Sea \\\"Black Sea\\\") as part of the [Constantinople Flotilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constantinople_Flotilla \\\"Constantinople Flotilla\\\"). The U-boat sank one ship of 6,011 [GRT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage \\\"Gross register tonnage\\\") in September 1915. In October, she helped repel a Russian bombardment of Bulgaria. She was considered for transfer to the [Bulgarian Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bulgarian_Navy \\\"Bulgarian Navy\\\"), but disappeared in late September 1916 before a transfer could take place. Her fate is officially unknown, but sources report that may have struck a mine or been sunk by a Russian airplane. \\n## Design and construction\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=SM_UB-7&action=edit&section=1 \\\"Edit section: Design and construction\\\")]\\nAfter the [German Army](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Army_\\\\(German_Empire\\\\) \\\"German Army \\\\(German Empire\\\\)\\\")'s rapid advance along the North Sea coast in the earliest stages of World War I, the [German Imperial Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Imperial_Navy \\\"German Imperial Navy\\\") found itself without suitable submarines that could be operated in the narrow and shallow seas off [Flanders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flanders \\\"Flanders\\\").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Miller-46-1)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Karau-48-10) Project 34, a design effort begun in mid-August 1914,[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Karau-48-10) produced the [Type UB I](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Type_UB_I_submarine \\\"Type UB I submarine\\\") design: a small submarine that could be shipped by rail to a port of operations and quickly assembled. Constrained by railroad size limitations, the UB I design called for a boat about 28 metres (92 ft) long and displacing about 125 tonnes (123 long tons) with two [torpedo tubes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torpedo_tube \\\"Torpedo tube\\\").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Miller-46-1)[[Note 2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-11) _UB-7_ was part of the initial allotment of eight submarines\\u2014numbered [_UB-1_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_U-10_\\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\) \\\"SM U-10 \\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\)\\\") to [_UB-8_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-8 \\\"SM UB-8\\\")\\u2014ordered on 15 October from [Germaniawerft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Germaniawerft \\\"Germaniawerft\\\") of [Kiel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kiel \\\"Kiel\\\"), just shy of two months after planning for the class began.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Miller-46-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Williamson-2)\\n_UB-7_ was [laid down](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keel_laying \\\"Keel laying\\\") by Germaniawerft in Kiel on 30 November.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB-7-4) As built, _UB-7_ was 28.10 metres (92 ft 2 in) long, 3.15 metres (10 ft 4 in) [abeam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beam_\\\\(nautical\\\\) \\\"Beam \\\\(nautical\\\\)\\\"), and had a [draft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Draft_\\\\(ship\\\\) \\\"Draft \\\\(ship\\\\)\\\") of 3.03 metres (9 ft 11 in). She had a single 59-brake-horsepower (44 kW) [Daimler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daimler-Motoren-Gesellschaft \\\"Daimler-Motoren-Gesellschaft\\\") 4-cylinder [diesel engine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diesel_engine \\\"Diesel engine\\\") for surface travel, and a single 119-shaft-horsepower (89 kW) [Siemens-Schuckert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siemens-Schuckert \\\"Siemens-Schuckert\\\") [electric motor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_motor \\\"Electric motor\\\") for underwater travel, both attached to a single [propeller shaft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Propeller_shaft_\\\\(ship\\\\) \\\"Propeller shaft \\\\(ship\\\\)\\\"). Her top speeds were 6.47 knots (11.98 km/h; 7.45 mph), surfaced, and 5.51 knots (10.20 km/h; 6.34 mph), submerged.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Tarrant-172-3) At more moderate speeds, she could sail up to 1,650 [nautical miles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nautical_mile \\\"Nautical mile\\\") (3,060 km; 1,900 mi) on the surface before refueling, and up to 45 nautical miles (83 km; 52 mi) submerged before recharging her batteries. Like all boats of the class, _UB-7_ was rated to a diving depth of 50 metres (160 ft), and could completely submerge in 33 seconds. \\n_UB-7_ was armed with two 45-centimeter (17.7 in) [torpedoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torpedo \\\"Torpedo\\\") in two bow [torpedo tubes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torpedo_tube \\\"Torpedo tube\\\"). She was also outfitted for a single 8-millimeter (0.31 in) [machine gun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Machine_gun \\\"Machine gun\\\") on deck. _UB-7_ 's standard complement consisted of one officer and thirteen enlisted men.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Karau-49-12)\\nWhile _UB-7_ 's construction neared completion in early March 1915, [Enver Pasha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enver_Pasha \\\"Enver Pasha\\\") and other Turkish leaders were pleading with their German and [Austro-Hungarian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austria-Hungary \\\"Austria-Hungary\\\") allies to send submarines to the Dardanelles to help attack the British and French fleet pounding Turkish positions.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-116-13) The Germans induced the [Austro-Hungarian Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Navy \\\"Austro-Hungarian Navy\\\") ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language \\\"German language\\\"): _Kaiserliche und K\\u00f6nigliche Kriegsmarine_ or _K.u.K. Kriegsmarine_) to send two boats\\u2014its own Germaniawerft-built boats [_U-3_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_U-3_\\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\) \\\"SM U-3 \\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\)\\\") and [_U-4_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_U-4_\\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\) \\\"SM U-4 \\\\(Austria-Hungary\\\\)\\\")\\u2014with the promise of _UB-7_ and _UB-8_ as replacements.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-14)\\nWhen work on _UB-7_ and _UB-8_ was complete at the Germaniwerft yard, they were both readied for rail shipment. The process of shipping a UB I boat involved breaking the submarine down into what was essentially a [knock down kit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complete_knock_down \\\"Complete knock down\\\"). Each boat was broken into approximately fifteen pieces and loaded onto eight railway [flatcars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flatcar \\\"Flatcar\\\").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Karau-49-12) The boats were ready for shipment to the main Austrian naval base at [Pola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pula \\\"Pula\\\") on 15 March, despite the fact that the Austrian pair was still not ready.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-14) German engineers and technicians that accompanied the German boats to Pola worked under the supervision of [Kapit\\u00e4nleutnant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapit%C3%A4nleutnant \\\"Kapit\\u00e4nleutnant\\\") Hans Adam, head of the newly created U-boat special command ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language \\\"German language\\\"): _Sonderkommando_).[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-14) Typically, the UB I assembly process took about two to three weeks,[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Karau-49-12) and, accordingly, _UB-7_ was [launched](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Launch_\\\\(ship\\\\) \\\"Launch \\\\(ship\\\\)\\\") at Pola sometime in April.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Miramar-5)\\n## Career\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=SM_UB-7&action=edit&section=2 \\\"Edit section: Career\\\")]\\nDuring her trials, _UB-7_ developed a leak which took some time to repair.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-83-15) In the meantime, she was assigned the Austrian number of _U-7_ and an Austrian commander.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-83-15)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Con-341-16) Her German crew at Pola\\u2014since it was still the intent for _UB-7_ to be transferred to the _K.u.K. Kriegsmarine_ \\u2014wore either civilian clothes or Austrian uniforms.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Kob-82-83-15) As time dragged on, the Austrian _U-3_ and _U-4_ were still not ready,[[Note 3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-17) and eventually Admiral [Anton Haus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anton_Haus \\\"Anton Haus\\\"), the head of the Austrian Navy, reneged on his commitment because of the overt hostility from neighbor and former ally Italy.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-116-13)[[Note 4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-18)\\nWith the change of heart from the Austrians, Germany resolved to retain _UB-7_ and send her to the aid of the Turks.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Sond-268-19) So, upon completion of her leak repairs, the boat was [commissioned](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ship_commissioning \\\"Ship commissioning\\\") into the German Imperial Navy as SM _UB-7_ on 6 May under the command of [Oberleutnant zur See](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oberleutnant_zur_See \\\"Oberleutnant zur See\\\") [Wilhelm Werner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilhelm_Werner \\\"Wilhelm Werner\\\"),[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB-7-4) a 26-year-old native of [Apolda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apolda \\\"Apolda\\\").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-werner-7)[[Note 5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-20) At commissioning, the boat temporarily joined the [Pola Flotilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pola_Flotilla \\\"Pola Flotilla\\\") ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language \\\"German language\\\"): _Deutsche U-Halbflotille Pola_).[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB-7-4)\\nBecause of her limited range, _UB-7_ would not have been able to make the entire journey to Turkey, so on the night on 15/16 May, she was towed by the Austrian destroyer [SMS  _Triglav_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SMS_Triglav_\\\\(1913\\\\) \\\"SMS Triglav \\\\(1913\\\\)\\\") through the [Straits of Otranto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Straits_of_Otranto \\\"Straits of Otranto\\\") and into the [Ionian Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ionian_Sea \\\"Ionian Sea\\\").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Sond-268-19)[[Note 6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-21) By June,[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-22) _UB-7_ had reached [Smyrna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C4%B0zmir \\\"\\u0130zmir\\\")\\u2014not having any success on her journey there[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-118-23)\\u2014and joined [_U-21_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_U-21_\\\\(Germany\\\\) \\\"SM U-21 \\\\(Germany\\\\)\\\") and _UB-8_ in the [Constantinople Flotilla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constantinople_Flotilla \\\"Constantinople Flotilla\\\") ([German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language \\\"German language\\\"): _U-boote der Mittelmeer division in Konstantinopel_).[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Tarrant-23-24) Once there, _UB-7_ was ineffective because she was hampered by her limited torpedo supply and her weak engines, which made negotiating the strong Dardanelles currents nearly impossible.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-118-23) Because of this, _UB-7_ was sent to patrol in the [Black Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Sea \\\"Black Sea\\\") in July, cruising without success from the 5th to the 22nd.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-233-25)\\nIn September 1915, _UB-7_ and _UB-8_ were sent to [Varna, Bulgaria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Varna,_Bulgaria \\\"Varna, Bulgaria\\\"), and from there, to patrol off the Russian Black Sea coast. On 18 September, _UB-7_ torpedoed and sank the British steamer _Patagonia_ about 10.5 nautical miles (19.4 km; 12.1 mi) from [Odessa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odessa \\\"Odessa\\\").[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Tenn-190-26)[[Note 7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-27) The cargo ship, of 6,011 [gross register tons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gross_register_tonnage \\\"Gross register tonnage\\\") (GRT),[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Tenn-190-26) was the only ship credited to _UB-7_ ,[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-UB7-ships-28) and the only one sunk by any of the Constantinople Flotilla in the month.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Tarr-148-29)\\nBecause [Bulgaria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bulgaria \\\"Bulgaria\\\") had joined the [Central Powers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Powers \\\"Central Powers\\\"), battleships of the Russian [Black Sea Fleet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Sea_Fleet \\\"Black Sea Fleet\\\"), and aircraft from the [seaplane carriers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seaplane_carrier \\\"Seaplane carrier\\\") [_Almaz_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_cruiser_Almaz \\\"Russian cruiser Almaz\\\") and [_Imperator Nikolai I_](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Russian_seaplane_carrier_Imperator_Nikolai_I&action=edit&redlink=1 \\\"Russian seaplane carrier Imperator Nikolai I \\\\(page does not exist\\\\)\\\") began attacks on Varna and the Bulgarian coast on 25 October. _UB-7_ and _UB-8_ , both based out of [Varna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Varna,_Bulgaria \\\"Varna, Bulgaria\\\") by this time, sortied to disrupt the bombardment.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-236-30) Off Varna on the 27th, _UB-7_ got in position to fire a torpedo at the [_Panteleimon_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_battleship_Potemkin \\\"Russian battleship Potemkin\\\") (most well-known under her former name of _Potemkin_).[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-GP-73-31) Although _UB-7_ 's crew heard what they thought was the torpedo explode, it did not hit _Panteleimon_. Despite the lack of success, the attempt did cause the Russians to break off their attacks and withdraw.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-236-30)\\nIn early 1916, _UB-7_ and _UB-8_ were still cruising in the Black Sea out of Varna.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-GP-124%E2%80%9325-32) The Germans did not have good luck in the Black Sea, which was not a priority for them.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-233-25) The Bulgarians, who saw the value of the submarines in repelling Russian attacks, began negotiations to purchase _UB-7_ and _UB-8_.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Miller-46-1) Bulgarian sailors practiced in the pair of boats and technicians were sent to [Kiel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kiel \\\"Kiel\\\") for training at the German submarine school there.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Panayotov-33)[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Jordanov-34) The transfer of _UB-8_ to the [Bulgarian Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bulgarian_Navy \\\"Bulgarian Navy\\\") took place on 25 May 1916,[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Jordanov-34) but for reasons unreported in sources, _UB-7_ remained under the German flag. \\nIn July 1916, the Germans sent [SMS  _Breslau_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SMS_Breslau \\\"SMS Breslau\\\") to mine off [Novorossisk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novorossisk \\\"Novorossisk\\\").[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-246-35) To attempt to neutralize any Russian response, _UB-7_ \\u2014under the command of Hans L\\u00fctjohann, who had taken over for Werner when he returned to Germany to command the new [_U-55_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_U-55 \\\"SM U-55\\\")[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-werner-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Lutjohann-8)\\u2014was stationed off [Sevastopol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sevastopol \\\"Sevastopol\\\") to attack any ships that sailed in response to the mission.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-246-35) Unfortunately, Russian seaplanes spotted _UB-7_ and bombed the U-boat, preventing her from accomplishing her goal. With the submarine out of the way, Rear Admiral [Aleksandr Kolchak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleksandr_Kolchak \\\"Aleksandr Kolchak\\\") sortied with [dreadnought](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dreadnought \\\"Dreadnought\\\") [_Imperatritsa Mariya_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_battleship_Imperatritsa_Mariya \\\"Russian battleship Imperatritsa Mariya\\\"), cruiser _[Kagul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_cruiser_Ochakov_\\\\(1902\\\\) \\\"Russian cruiser Ochakov \\\\(1902\\\\)\\\")_ , and five destroyers. The Russian fleet engaged _Breslau_ , which was forced to abort her mission and retire. Sources are quiet on damage, if any, suffered by _UB-7_.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_UB-7#cite_note-Halp-246-35)\\n## Summary of raiding history\\n[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=SM_UB-7&action=edit&section=3 \\\"Edit section: Summary of raiding history\\\")]\\nDate  | Name  | Nationality  | Tonnage[[Note [_Patagonia_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_shipwrecks_in_September_1915#15_September \\\"List of shipwrecks in September 1915\\\") |
